concept_name: Position-Time Graphs — Reading Motion From a Line on Paper
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 7.5 — Graphical representation of motion; split from motion_graphs bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: The route-map of a Delhi Metro train from Rajiv Chowk to Central Secretariat. On a graph where x is position (kilometres from Rajiv Chowk) and t is time (seconds), the train's journey draws a curve. When the train is stationary at a platform, the curve is a horizontal line. When it is accelerating away, the curve bends upward. When it cruises at full speed, the line rises steadily. When it brakes into the next station, the curve bends back toward horizontal. The shape of the line tells you everything about the train's motion, without ever knowing the acceleration equation.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A GPS tracker on a delivery scooter in Bengaluru, logging position every second. Plot x-position against time — you get a continuous curve. A rapid climb on the curve means the scooter is moving fast. A shallow slope means slow. A flat segment means the scooter is idling at a traffic light. A backward segment means the rider turned around. Reading these shapes is a life skill for every physics student.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A film projector running at constant speed. If you plot the position of the film strip at each frame, you get a straight line going upward at a constant slope. That straight-line shape IS uniform motion. Every bending or flattening on an x-t graph is a story about how the motion is changing.

curvature_sign.name: Sign of curvature = sign of acceleration

formulas.slope_is_velocity: slope of x-t graph at a point = dx/dt = velocity at that point
formulas.tangent_slope: Instantaneous velocity = slope of the tangent at that point
formulas.average_slope: Average velocity between t1 and t2 = slope of the chord connecting (t1, x1) to (t2, x2)
formulas.curvature_is_accel_sign: Curving upward -> acceleration positive. Curving downward -> acceleration negative. Straight line -> zero acceleration.

constraints: Straight sloping line -> constant velocity (non-zero v, zero a)
Upward-bending curve -> speeding up if v > 0, slowing down if v < 0
A positive x at any t means object is on the + side of origin; a negative x means object is on the - side

STATE_1.title: The Graph as a Diary of Motion

scene_composition.text:
Position-Time graph (x vs t)

x |              /
  |            /
  |         /
  |      /     <- rising fast
  |   /
  | /            <- shallow rise
  |_______
  |         flat segment (at rest)
  |_______
  |
  |__________________  t
  0

  Reading the shape
    Flat     -> object at rest (v = 0)
    Shallow  -> slow motion (small v)
    Steep    -> fast motion (large v)

Big idea

An x-t graph is a full record
of WHERE an object has been at
every moment.

The SHAPE of the graph encodes
every other quantity:
  velocity (slope at each point)
  acceleration (curvature)

You can solve many kinematics
questions just by reading the graph.
No equations needed.
The Graph as a Diary of Motion

tts_sentences.text_en: An x-t graph is a complete diary of motion. For every moment on the time axis, the curve tells you exactly where the object was.
Every physical quantity you care about is encoded in the shape. Velocity is the slope at each point. Acceleration is the curvature.
A flat segment means the object is not moving — position is unchanged across that stretch of time. A shallow slope means slow motion. A steep slope means fast motion.
You can solve most kinematics problems by looking at the graph alone. No equations needed if you know how to read the shape.
The next two states teach you the two visual skills that matter — reading velocity from slope, and reading acceleration from curvature.

STATE_2.title: Slope = Velocity — The First Reading Skill

scene_composition.text:
x | slope = dx/dt = velocity
  |
  |        / <- tangent at t = 3
  |       /
  |      .  (point at t=3, x=10)
  |    /
  |  /
  |_______________
  |
  |__________________  t
  0                       t = 3

  At t = 3 s:
  tangent slope = 5 m/s
  => instantaneous velocity is 5 m/s

Chord from (0, 0) to (3, 10)
  chord slope = 10 / 3 = 3.33 m/s
  => average velocity 0-3 s = 3.33 m/s

Slope rules

Steep positive slope   -> large positive v
Shallow positive slope -> small positive v
Zero slope (horizontal)-> v = 0 (at rest)
Negative slope         -> v < 0 (moving in -x direction)

At a single instant, read the
TANGENT slope. That is instantaneous v.

Between two instants, read the
CHORD slope. That is average v.
Slope = Velocity — The First Reading Skill

tts_sentences.text_en: The first reading skill — slope of the curve at any point equals velocity at that moment.
Look at any single point on the curve. Draw the tangent line there. Its slope is the instantaneous velocity.
If you want velocity at a single instant, use the tangent. If you want the average velocity between two instants, use the chord — the straight line joining the two points on the curve.
Steep positive slope means high positive velocity. Shallow positive slope means slow positive motion. Flat means at rest. Negative slope means the object is moving in the negative x direction.
This one skill — reading velocity off the slope — handles a huge fraction of kinematics graph questions. Practice it on every x-t graph you see.

STATE_3.title: Curvature = Acceleration — The Second Reading Skill

scene_composition.text:
Three distinct x-t shapes

A.  /  straight line (constant v)
     /              a = 0
    /

B.       /  concave up
      /              a > 0 (speeding up or
    /                    slowing down in -v dir)
  /

C.  \
     \  concave down
      \              a < 0 (slowing down or
       \                    speeding up in -v dir)
        \

Reading curvature in 1 second:
  Straight -> zero acceleration.
  Bends upward -> positive acceleration.
  Bends downward -> negative acceleration.

Is it speeding up or slowing down?

Same sign of v and curvature -> speeding up.
Opposite signs -> slowing down.

Example 1  v > 0 (rising curve),
            curvature > 0 (concave up)
            -> SAME sign -> speeding up.

Example 2  v > 0 (rising curve),
            curvature < 0 (concave down)
            -> OPPOSITE -> slowing down.

Same rule as the sign_convention concept:
sign(v) * sign(a) > 0 -> speeding up.
Curvature = Acceleration — The Second Reading Skill

tts_sentences.text_en: The second reading skill — curvature tells you the sign of acceleration.
A straight-line x-t graph has zero curvature, so acceleration is zero. The object moves at constant velocity.
An x-t graph that bends upward — concave up — has positive curvature, so acceleration is positive.
An x-t graph that bends downward — concave down — has negative curvature, so acceleration is negative.
To tell speeding up from slowing down, check whether the signs of velocity and acceleration match. If yes, speeding up. If opposite, slowing down. Same rule we learned in the sign convention concept.
So on a position-time graph, slope tells you velocity and its sign. Curvature tells you acceleration and its sign. Between them, you can describe any motion without writing a single formula.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The x-t graph shows the path the object traces in space
trigger_phrases: does the object actually move along this graph

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — The Graph IS the Trajectory

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

The x-t graph for a thrown ball
looks like a parabola.

I know the ball flies in a parabolic
arc in space.

So the graph IS the path of the ball,
right?

(No. The graph is a POSITION-VERSUS-TIME
PLOT. It can look parabolic for motion
that moves only UP and DOWN — no
actual arc in space.)
Your Belief — The Graph IS the Trajectory
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Students often confuse an x-t graph with the actual spatial path of the object.
The trap is that for some motions — for example, a ball thrown straight up and falling back — the x-t graph IS a parabola, and the real path is also 'up and down' which looks arc-like.
But these are two different things. The graph is a plot of position versus time. The physical path is the actual trace in space.

STATE_2.title: A Counter-Example — Straight-Line Graph for Straight Motion

scene_composition.text:
Simple case
A car drives due east on a straight road at 60 km/h.

Real path: a straight line on the ground.
x-t graph: a straight line with slope 60.

The graph looks like a line, the path is a line.
Easy to confuse them here.

Motion of a ball thrown up and falling
  Real path: up and straight back down
             (one-dimensional segment)
  x-t graph: y starts at 0, rises to peak
             at mid-time, falls back to 0
             - a parabola.

SAME motion, graph shape is parabola,
path shape is a single line.

The graph is a RELATIONSHIP of x to t.
It is NOT a physical picture of where
the object went.
A Counter-Example — Straight-Line Graph for Straight Motion
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Consider the clearest counter-example. A ball thrown straight up from the ground and falling back to the ground.
The physical path is a single line — up and straight back down, along the same vertical line in space.
But the x-t graph is a parabola. y starts at zero, rises to the peak at half the total time, and falls back to zero.
The graph SHAPE is a parabola. The physical path is a line. Same motion, two very different pictures.
The graph is a relationship between x and t. It is NOT a physical picture of where the object went. Keep this distinction, and you will not confuse a graph shape for a real trajectory.

STATE_3.title: When the Graph DOES Look Like the Path

scene_composition.text:
Exception: 1D motion at constant velocity

Object moves along x-axis at v = 5 m/s.
Real path: a line on the x-axis.
x-t graph: a line with slope 5.

Both are straight lines. But even here,
they represent different things:
  Path line has no 'length' dimension of time.
  Graph line has x on one axis, t on the other.

Rule of thumb
  Never assume 'graph shape = path shape'.
  Always ask: what do the axes represent?
  What does SLOPE mean here? CURVATURE?
  Interpret the shape through the axes.
When the Graph DOES Look Like the Path
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: There is one case where the graph accidentally looks like the path — one-dimensional motion at constant velocity. Both the physical path and the x-t graph are straight lines.
But even there, they represent different things. The path line is geometric — a set of points in space. The graph line has a time axis and a position axis, with slope that represents velocity.

The rule of thumb — never assume graph shape equals path shape. Always ask: what do the axes represent? What does slope mean? What does curvature mean? Interpret the graph through the axes, not through visual resemblance.

regeneration_variants.label: Multi-segment graph
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Train graph: flat (at platform), then straight rising (uniform motion), then concave-up (accelerating), then concave-down (braking). Read each segment and identify velocity/acceleration.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same rules apply piece by piece

regeneration_variants.label: Reversal on the x-t graph
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Graph with a peak (x rises then falls). Object reverses direction at the peak. At the peak, tangent slope = 0, so instantaneous velocity is zero.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Reversal = tangent slope passes through zero
